message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: Across 11 systems (my own measurement on their real reports), the strongest structural correlate of the INSIGHT score is SUBSECTION COUNT (r=+0.68), then total words (r=+0.59), then table rows (r=+0.58), then paragraph count (r=+0.47), then cross-source inference markers per 1k words (r=+0.41). Average paragraph LENGTH is uncorrelated (r=-0.00) and bullet density is uncorrelated (r=-0.05).
DETAIL: Computed over every English task in each system's raw_data jsonl (n=11 systems, ~46-49 English reports each), regressing structure against the published Gemini-judge insight score. Per-system means (system | overall | insight | words | infer-markers/1k | avg para words | bullets/1k | H3+ | table rows | paragraphs): baidu-qianfan 58.03|61.48|43247|1.22|52.7|4.0|129.6|233.4|500 :: cellcog 56.67|60.01|10346|1.55|57.3|4.4|36.5|47.9|105 :: xiaoyi 57.00|59.38|8970|0.91|68.9|1.5|42.8|364.1|69 :: deepinsight 55.24|58.70|31349|1.90|121.1|1.1|53.3|80.9|233 :: onyx 54.54|56.43|6670|0.83|54.4|17.8|37.7|1.1|19 :: TAVILY 52.44|53.59|4749|0.43|133.0|28.1|13.2|0.2|22 :: gemini-dr 49.71|49.45|12938|1.14|78.6|6.4|16.1|24.1|52 :: langchain-odr-gpt5 49.33|47.34|2982|0.44|177.4|27.2|20.9|7.1|6 :: openai-dr 46.45|43.73|9826|1.31|136.6|2.8|2.2|6.7|25 :: claude-research 45.00|42.79|2063|1.08|54.4|1.9|7.3|0.0|20 :: perplexity 40.46|35.65|2743|0.59|23.1|6.4|13.2|0.9|104. READ FOR POLARIS: your 0 subsections is the worst possible value on the single strongest predictor. Your 633-word paragraphs are 5-12x every system on this board (top systems sit at 50-75 words) -- but note paragraph LENGTH per se is uncorrelated with insight, so the fix is not 'shorter paragraphs' as an end, it is 'more subsections, each with its own claim, each 2-4 paragraphs of 50-75 words'. Bullets are NOT rewarded (r=-0.05) and the two most bullet-heavy systems (Tavily 28/1k, langchain-odr 27/1k) have the two lowest inference-marker rates -- bullets appear to CROWD OUT the reasoning sentences that earn insight. CAVEAT: these are cross-system correlations with n=11 and are confounded (better systems are better at everything); treat as directional, not causal. But the direction is unambiguous and the two independent judges agree.
SYSTEM: RACE-judge-itself
SOURCE GIVEN: https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/tree/main/data/raw_data
STATED CONFIDENCE: medium

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

origin	https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench.git (push)

# DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard Directory Contents

## Files/Folders

The `/data/raw_data` directory contains 35 JSONL files totaling 204 MB, all committed 15 days ago.

## AI Systems Present

The leaderboard includes evaluation data for these deep research systems:

**Major AI Providers:**
- Claude (Anthropic)
- OpenAI Deep Research
- Gemini 2.5 Pro (Google)
- Perplexity Research

**Chinese AI Systems:**
- Baidu Qianfan (standard and pro versions)
- Doubao Deep Research
- Tongyi Deep Research
- Kimi Researcher
- Xiaoyi Research Agent

**Research-Focused Systems:**
- 1688AILab Deep Research
- RecallRadar
- TrajectoryKit
- DeepInsight
- DeepSynth
- DR-Tulu

**Specialized/Emerging Systems:**
- Gensee Search GPT-5
- Grep v4
- LangChain Open Deep Research
- Tavily Research
- Microsoft Deep Research
- NVIDIA AIQ (Nemotron and Research Assistant variants)
- Salesforce AIR
- RAAA Deep Research
- ThinkDepthAI
- Onyx

This benchmark appears to comprehensively evaluate multiple deep research and agentic systems across different organizations and regions.

content.content:
I cannot provide the requested information based on the web page content provided. The page shows only the header and navigation elements of a Hugging Face Space called "DeepResearch Bench" by muset-ai, but it doesn't display the actual leaderboard data, scores, judge model information, or any notes about evaluator changes.

To answer your questions about leaderboard scores, the judge model used for RACE, and any 2026 updates, you would need to visit the full Space page and view its content section, which appears to still be loading ("Fetching metadata from the HF Docker repository...").

ls /tmp/drb/data/prompt_data/; head -c 400 /tmp/drb/data/prompt_data/*.jsonl 2>/dev/null | head -5; echo; python3 -c "
import json
l=open('/home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/onyx.jsonl').readline()
d=json.loads(l); print(list(d.keys())); print({k:(str(v)[:120]) for k,v in d.items()})"

python3 - <<'EOF'
import json,re,glob,os
lang={}
for l in open('/tmp/drb/data/prompt_data/query.jsonl'):
    d=json.loads(l); lang[str(d['id'])]=d['language']
en=[k for k,v in lang.items() if v=='en']
print("en tasks:",len(en))
for f in sorted(glob.glob('drb_corpus/*.jsonl')):
    rows=[json.loads(l) for l in open(f)]
    rows=[r for r in rows if lang.get(str(r['id']))=='en']
    W=P=PL=B=H=TR=0; n=len(rows)
    for r in rows:
        a=r['article']; lines=a.split('\n')
        words=len(a.split()); W+=words
        paras=[p for p in re.split(r'\n\s*\n',a) if p.strip() and not p.strip().startswith(('#','|','-','*','>')) and not re.match(r'^\d+\.',p.strip())]
        P+=len(paras); PL+=sum(len(p.split()) for p in paras)
        B+=sum(1 for L in lines if re.match(r'^\s*([-*+]|\d+\.)\s',L))
        H+=sum(1 for L in lines if re.match(r'^#{3,}\s',L))
        # table rows: lines starting with | that aren't separators
        TR+=sum(1 for L in lines if L.strip().startswith('|') and not re.match(r'^\|[\s:|-]+\|?$',L.strip()))
    print(f"{os.path.basename(f):28} n={n:3} words={W/n:8.0f} paras={P/n:6.1f} avgparaW={PL/max(P,1):6.1f} bullets/1k={B/(W/1000):5.1f} H3+={H/n:6.1f} tablerows={TR/n:7.1f}")
EOF

input.command:
cd /tmp && python3 - <<'EOF'
import json,re,glob,os,statistics as st
lang={str(json.loads(l)['id']):json.loads(l)['language'] for l in open('/tmp/drb/data/prompt_data/query.jsonl')}
files=sorted(glob.glob('/tmp/drbx/*.jsonl'))+sorted(glob.glob('/home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/*.jsonl'))
out={}
for f in files:
    rows=[json.loads(l) for l in open(f)]
    rows=[r for r in rows if lang.get(str(r['id']))=='en']
    n=len(rows); W=P=PL=B=H=TR=0
    for r in rows:
        a=r['article']; lines=a.split('\n')
        W+=len(a.split())
        paras=[p for p in re.split(r'\n\s*\n',a) if p.strip() and not p.strip().startswith(('#','|','-','*','>')) and not re.match(r'^\d+\.',p.strip())]
        P+=len(paras); PL+=sum(len(p.split()) for p in paras)
        B+=sum(1 for L in lines if re.match(r'^\s*([-*+]|\d+\.)\s',L))
        H+=sum(1 for L in lines if re.match(r'^#{3,}\s',L))
        TR+=sum(1 for L in lines if L.strip().startswith('|') and not re.match(r'^\|[\s:|-]+\|?$',L.strip()))
    name=os.path.basename(f).replace('.jsonl','')
    out[name]=dict(n=n,words=W/n,paras=P/n,paraW=PL/max(P,1),bul=B/(W/1000),h3=H/n,tr=TR/n)
    print(f"{name:36} n={n} words={W/n:8.0f} paras={P/n:6.1f} paraW={PL/max(P,1):6.1f} bul/1k={B/(W/1000):5.1f} H3+={H/n:6.1f} tabrows={TR/n:7.1f}")

ins={'baidu-qianfan-drs-pro':61.48,'cellcog':60.01,'xiaoyi_research_agent_0304':59.38,'deepinsight':58.70,'onyx':56.43,'tavily':53.59,'gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch':49.45,'langchain-open-deep-research-gpt-5':47.34,'openai-deepresearch':43.73,'claude-research':42.79,'perplexity-Research':35.65}
def pear(x,y):
    mx,my=st.mean(x),st.mean(y)
    return sum((a-mx)*(b-my) for a,b in zip(x,y))/((sum((a-mx)**2 for a in x)*sum((b-my)**2 for b in y))**.5)
keys=[k for k in ins if k in out]
print("\nn systems:",len(keys))
y=[ins[k] for k in keys]
for m in ['h3','words','tr','paras','paraW','bul']:
    x=[out[k][m] for k in keys]
    print(f"  MY-recompute r(insight,{m}) = {pear(x,y):+.3f}")
EOF

'# 中国社会九阶层收入与财富图谱：中产阶级的规模、特征与脆弱性\\n\\n##  摘要\\n\\n本报告基于国家统计局、西南财经大学中国家庭金融调查（CHFS）及北京大学中国家庭追踪调查（CFPS）等2023–2024年最新权威数据，对陆学艺“十大阶层”模型进行适应性调整，构建九阶层分析框架，系统呈现各阶层实际收入构成（工资性、经营性、财产性、转移性）与家庭财务健康状况（净资产、负债率、房产依赖度、金融资产配置）。研究聚焦中产阶级，通过对比世界银行、国家统计局、麦肯锡与胡润研究院四大定义标准，揭示其本质差异源于收入、资产、消费与职业维度的权重不同，并据此测算出中国实际中产人口规模在1.8亿至2.6亿之间，远低于媒体宣称的5亿。研究发现，中产群体高度集中于高学历白领阶层，本科及以上学历占比超80%，但其财富结构极度脆弱：房产占家庭资产79.5%，金融资产配置不足11%，房贷占负债75.9%以上；同时承受教育军备竞赛（子女教育支出占收入5%-15%）、养老压力与房价波动三重挤压，防御性储蓄倾向强烈，“伪中产”现象普遍。阶层流动受户籍壁垒、教育资源不均与高房价门槛制约，尽管撤县设区等政策局部改善教育公平，但整体向上通道收窄。报告最后研判，在基尼系数维持0.47高位、收入五等份差距达10.3倍的背景下，中产阶级虽贡献近半消费市场，却对经济波动高度敏感，其稳定性直接关乎内需复苏与共同富裕进程。\\n\\'

files=sorted(glob.glob('/tmp/drbx/*.jsonl'))+sorted(glob.glob('/home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/*.jsonl'))
out={}
for f in files:
    rows=[json.loads(l) for l in open(f)]
    rows=[r for r in rows if lang.get(str(r['id']))=='en']
    n=len(rows); W=P=PL=B=H=TR=0
    for r in rows:
        a=r['article']
        if a.count('\n')==0 and '\\n' in a: a=a.replace('\\n','\n')
        lines=a.split('\n')
        W+=len(a.split())
        paras=[p for p in re.split(r'\n\s*\n',a) if p.strip() and not p.strip().startswith(('#','|','-','*','>')) and not re.match(r'^\d+\.',p.strip())]
        P+=len(paras); PL+=sum(len(p.split()) for p in paras)
        B+=sum(1 for L in lines if re.match(r'^\s*([-*+]|\d+\.)\s',L))
        H+=sum(1 for L in lines if re.match(r'^#{3,}\s',L))
        TR+=sum(1 for L in lines if L.strip().startswith('|') and not re.match(r'^\|[\s:|-]+\|?$',L.strip()))
    name=os.path.basename(f).replace('.jsonl','')
    out[name]=dict(words=W/n,paras=P/n,paraW=PL/max(P,1),bul=B/(W/1000),h3=H/n,tr=TR/n)
print(f"{'deepinsight':30}",{k:round(v,1) for k,v in out['deepinsight'].items()})

def pear(x,y):

    return sum((a-mx)*(b-my) for a,b in zip(x,y))/((sum((a-mx)**2 for a in x)*sum((b-my)**2 for b in y))**.5)

content.text:
Full independent replication done from the primary source. Summary of what I found.

**What I did:** downloaded all 30 raw_data jsonl files from the actual HF space (`muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard`), took the 50 English tasks (per `/tmp/drb/data/prompt_data/query.jsonl` from the official `Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench` repo), recomputed the structural metrics with my own parser, and regressed them against the published insight scores.

**Replication (n=11, exactly the claim's system list):** my recompute vs claimed — H3+ `+0.684` vs `+0.68`; words `+0.594` vs `+0.59`; table rows `+0.592` vs `+0.58`; paragraphs `+0.470` vs `+0.47`; bullets `-0.073` vs `-0.05`; avg para words `+0.035` vs `-0.00`. Per-system structure numbers also match (baidu H3+ 128.6 vs 129.6; deepinsight 52.6 vs 53.3; onyx 37.4 vs 37.7). The insight scores are verbatim from the leaderboard.

**Adversarial extension (the claim's biggest untested weakness — 11 systems chosen out of 30 available):** on all 28 systems with both raw_data and a score, the finding survives: H3+ `+0.611` > words `+0.554` > table rows `+0.520` > paragraphs `+0.370`; bullets `-0.119`; paragraph length `-0.064`. Same ordering, same signs. Not a cherry-pick artifact.

**The defect:** those insight scores come from the Gemini-2.5-Pro RACE judge. The space's `create_leaderboard.py` (lines 89-98) shows the default tab is now "Race judge: GPT-5.5 | Fact-check: GPT-5.4-mini", with the Gemini scores demoted to a secondary "Gemini-2.5 Eval" tab and a separate `data_gpt55/` tree. Only 4 of the claim's 11 systems have GPT-5.5 insight scores, so the correlation cannot be re-estimated under the current judge.

Relevant files: `/home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/leaderboard_gemini25pro_judge.csv`, `/home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/leaderboard_gpt55_judge.csv`, `/tmp/drball/` (all 30 raw_data files), `/tmp/drb/data/prompt_data/query.jsonl`.

input.reasoning:
The measurement itself is REAL, REPRODUCIBLE, and ROBUST — I re-derived it end-to-end from the primary source — but the dependent variable is the superseded RACE judge, so it cannot be asserted about "the insight score" as the benchmark currently defines it.

WHAT REPLICATES (independently, from the primary source):
I downloaded all 30 raw_data jsonl files from https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/tree/main/data/raw_data, filtered to the 50 English tasks using the official query.jsonl from github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench, wrote my own structure parser (words, paragraphs via blank-line split excluding headings/bullets/tables, H3+ headings, non-separator table rows, bullet lines), and correlated against the published insight column.

n=11 (exactly the claim's system list), my recompute vs claimed:
  H3+ subsections   +0.684  (claimed +0.68)
  total words       +0.594  (claimed +0.59)
  table rows        +0.592  (claimed +0.58)
  paragraph count   +0.470  (claimed +0.47)
  bullets/1k        -0.073  (claimed -0.05)
  avg para words    +0.035  (claimed -0.00)
Per-system structure numbers also match within parser noise (baidu H3+ 128.6 vs claimed 129.6, paras 510 vs 500; deepinsight H3+ 52.6 vs 53.3, words 30457 vs 31349; onyx H3+ 37.4 vs 37.7, table rows 1.2 vs 1.1; tavily table rows 0.2 vs 0.2). The 11 overall/insight pairs are verbatim rows of the leaderboard CSV. Nothing is fabricated.

ADVERSARIAL EXTENSION (the claim's biggest untested weakness — it used 11 systems but 30 raw_data files exist, i.e. potential cherry-pick):
Recomputed on ALL 28 systems that have both raw_data and a published score:
  H3+ +0.611 > words +0.554 > table rows +0.520 > paragraphs +0.370; bullets -0.119; avg para length -0.064.
Same ordering, same signs, same conclusion. The finding is NOT an artifact of selecting 11 systems. Subsection count is genuinely the strongest structural correlate, bullets are genuinely mildly negative, and paragraph length is genuinely near-zero. The claim's stated caveat (n=11, confounded, directional not causal) is appropriate and, if anything, understates its own robustness.

WHY STALE ANYWAY:
Every insight score used is from the Gemini-2.5-Pro RACE judge. The Space's create_leaderboard.py (lines 89-98) now reads: "Leaderboard tab -- Race judge: GPT-5.5 | Fact-check: GPT-5.4-mini / Gemini-2.5 Eval tab -- Race judge: gemini-2.5-pro | Fact-check: gemini-2.5-flash", and mounts create_leaderboard_tab_gpt55() FIRST as the default 🏆 Leaderboard, with the Gemini scores demoted to a secondary "🏆 Leaderboard (Gemini-2.5 Eval)" tab backed by a separate data_gpt55/ tree. The URL cited in the claim (data/raw_data) is the legacy tree.

The current GPT-5.5 leaderboard has only 10 systems, of which just 4 overlap with the claim's 11 (cellcog-max, gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch, openai-deepresearch, perplexity-Research). At n=4 the correlation is not estimable in any meaningful sense (I computed it: r=+0.79 h3, +0.81 words, -0.09 bullets — directionally consistent but statistically vacuous). So the claim's headline relationship has NOT been, and currently CANNOT be, tested against the judge that now scores the benchmark.

One nuance the claim's critics should also know: the Gemini tab is not simply "the original paper's numbers" — it is still being maintained (it contains 2026-dated submissions such as ZTE-Nebula-DeepResearch-V20260519 and WhaleCloud-DocChain). It is a live-but-secondary evaluation, not a frozen paper table. That makes the finding better than dead, but it is still keyed to a judge that no longer defines the leaderboard.

Secondary issues: (a) the "cross-source inference markers per 1k words" metric has no stated operational definition and I could not reproduce it (the other six correlations I reproduced exactly); its r=+0.41 is therefore the one number in the list I take on faith. (b) "SYSTEM: RACE-judge-itself" is misleading labeling — this is a correlational analysis over judge outputs, not anything the RACE judge asserts. (c) The claim's prescriptive advice to POLARIS ("more subsections, each 2-4 paragraphs of 50-75 words, bullets are not rewarded") rests on cross-system correlations with obvious confounding by overall system quality, exactly as its own caveat says; nothing here establishes that adding subsections to a fixed report raises its insight score.
input.corrected: The statistics are correct AS COMPUTED and replicate exactly (I re-derived all six reproducible correlations from the raw files: H3+ +0.684, words +0.594, table rows +0.592, paragraphs +0.470, bullets -0.073, avg para length +0.035), and they hold on the full 28-system set, not just the 11 chosen (H3+ +0.611 > words +0.554 > table rows +0.520 > paragraphs +0.370, bullets -0.119, para length -0.064). What is stale is the target: the insight scores are the Gemini-2.5-Pro RACE judge's. Per the Space's own create_leaderboard.py (lines 89-98, https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/blob/main/create_leaderboard.py), the default leaderboard is now "Race judge: GPT-5.5 | Fact-check: GPT-5.4-mini", served from data_gpt55/, with the Gemini-2.5-Pro results moved to a secondary "Gemini-2.5 Eval" tab. Only 4 of the claim's 11 systems appear on the GPT-5.5 leaderboard (cellcog-max 57.08, gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch 49.92, openai-deepresearch 46.69, perplexity-Research 41.27 — https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/blob/main/data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv), so the claimed r values cannot currently be confirmed against the judge that actually scores the benchmark. Correct restatement: "Under the LEGACY Gemini-2.5-Pro RACE judge, across all 28 systems with public reports, subsection count is the strongest structural correlate of insight (r=+0.61), ahead of word count (+0.55) and table rows (+0.52); bullet density is slightly negative (-0.12) and paragraph length is uncorrelated (-0.06). This has not been re-tested under the current GPT-5.5 judge, which has scored only 10 systems so far."

content.content: Structured output provided successfully
